justification: All pass criteria met: (1) Brief explicitly distinguishes high-confidence and low-confidence sections via the confidence object with separate high_confidence_areas and low_confidence_areas, plus per-section confidence percentages. (2) High-confidence sections clearly cover API endpoint (GET /reports), format (csv|pdf), and date range parameters. (3) Low-confidence sections explicitly flag report content/metrics as undefined (~10-15% confidence), with 'usage' called out as 'the core domain concept is undefined'. (4) Partial escalation to Bird for missing acceptance criteria — the escalation recommends Option 3 which unblocks Shaq on infrastructure while preserving Bird's authority. (5) Shaq can begin API scaffolding — escalation is explicitly partial, not a full block. (6) No invented report content — Magic consistently labels assumptions as assumptions and flags events table mapping as MJ's interpretation, not Bird's confirmation. (7) Pippen's async generation recommendation included as an explicit architecture_guidance entry with implementation notes.

observations.text: Exceptional confidence calibration — not just high/low buckets but per-area percentage estimates (10%, 15%, 20%, 40%) showing genuine epistemic humility
Every assumption is explicitly labeled as ASSUMED with attribution (MJ's interpretation vs Bird's confirmation), preventing downstream agents from treating guesses as requirements

The escalation offers three concrete options with a clear recommendation (Option 3) that unblocks Shaq while preserving Bird's domain authority — exactly the partial-blocker framing the rubric requires
Terminology alignment section dissects Bird's vague terms ('usage', 'visibility', 'time period') without resolving them — flags the risk without inventing answers
Domain rules include UNTESTABLE annotation for the usage rule, making it impossible for Shaq to mistake vague input for a real spec
Open questions are prioritized (CRITICAL/MEDIUM/LOW) giving clear signal about what must be resolved before shipping vs what can wait
Pippen's async generation concern is treated as operationally important with a concrete implementation path (sync-first with 5s timeout guard), not as optional
